title: Italian olive oil cake
subtitle: Orange and almond cake with extra-virgin olive oil
excerpt: A single-layer Italian cake made with extra-virgin olive oil rather than butter, fragrant with orange zest and ground almonds. The oil gives the crumb a moist, tender texture that stays soft for four days without refrigeration. Dust with icing sugar and serve in thin slices.
sourceNotes: Olive oil cakes are documented in Italian domestic cooking from the medieval period, reflecting the use of olive oil rather than butter in Southern European kitchens where butter was expensive or unavailable. Italian domestic cookbooks of the nineteenth century, available through the Internet Archive, document a simple olive-oil sponge with citrus zest and ground almonds as a standard household cake in olive-growing regions. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the chapter on cakes notes that oil-based cakes keep more moist than butter cakes due to the oil remaining liquid at room temperature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap in clingfilm and freeze. Defrost at room temperature overnight and dust with icing sugar before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps wrapped at room temperature for 4 days. The olive oil flavour becomes more pronounced on day two.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The almond flour can catch at high heat — rotate the tin at 25 minutes if one side is browning faster

glossaryTerms.definition: The distinctive moist, fine texture of a cake made with oil rather than butter. Because oil does not solidify at room temperature the way butter does, the crumb of an oil cake stays tender and moist for longer than a butter cake. The texture is slightly denser and less airy than a creamed-butter sponge but compensates with a keeping quality that improves over 24–48 hours.

content.text: This cake is Italian in the simplest sense: olive oil in a cake batter where a French or British recipe would use butter. The result is subtly different in texture and keeping quality, and the olive oil flavour is present in the background if you use a good extra-virgin. With orange zest and a small amount of ground almonds, it is fragrant and light-textured despite the oil. No creaming required.

Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Oil and line the tin. In a large bowl, whisk together the {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and slightly thickened, about 2 minutes. Whisk in the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and the zest from both {{orange}}s. Add the {{plain-flour}}, {{almond-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and fold together until the batter is smooth.
Pour the batter into the tin. Bake for 38–40 minutes until the edges pull away from the tin and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The characteristic
will feel slightly springy when pressed, not bouncy like a butter sponge. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Once completely cold, dust lightly with {{icing-sugar}}.
